description: This figure presents a comprehensive series of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images illustrating the evolution of deformation-induced microstructures in Fe-35Mn-6Al-0.1C low-density steel subjected to tensile loading at liquid nitrogen temperature (LNT) across strains from 3% to 55%. Subfigures (a-b) at 3% strain reveal planar slip features including dislocation pairs, nodes, multi-junctions, and stacking faults nucleating at annealed twin boundaries. At 10% strain (c-e), deformation twins with widths of approximately 5 nm initiate at annealed twin boundaries, accompanied by complex planar slip patterns. The 20% strain condition (f-i) shows the development of dislocation cell blocks and dislocation cells alongside sparse deformation twins, indicating competition between slip and twinning. Upon reaching 28% strain (j-o), the microstructure refines into cell blocks and exhibits microbands with widths ranging from 137 nm to 390 nm. At 38% (p-q) and 55% (r-t) strains, extensive primary and secondary deformation twins dominate the microstructure, with secondary twins containing abundant stacking faults. The sequence demonstrates that low temperature promotes early twin nucleation at 10% strain via stacking fault formation, contrasting with room temperature behavior, and reveals the progressive transition from dislocation-mediated plasticity to twinning-dominated deformation that underlies the steel's exceptional cryogenic work hardening.
